title: 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding
subtitle: A sensory grounding practice for anxiety and overwhelm
excerpt: The 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique: five things you can see, four you can feel, three you can hear, two you can smell, one you can taste. Two minutes. Interrupts the anxiety loop by redirecting attention to what is physically present.
sourceNotes: The 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique is widely used in cognitive behavioural therapy and trauma-informed practice. It is attributed variously to therapists working with anxiety and PTSD from the 1990s onwards and is now considered public domain within the therapeutic tradition.

practice.whenToUse: Use when anxiety is spiking, when you feel overwhelmed or dissociated, or as a quick reset at any point in the day.

content.text: A two-minute grounding practice for anxiety and overwhelm. The technique works by redirecting attention from the internal anxiety loop to what is physically present in the room. Effective, fast, and requires nothing.
Sit or stand. Take one slow breath. You are going to name five things using your senses in sequence. Go slowly. Name each one precisely. The specific chair in front of you, not a generic category.
5 things you can see. Look around and name five specific things. Not categories. Specific objects. The crack in the ceiling. The mug on the desk. The colour of the wall in this particular light.
4 things you can physically feel. The fabric of your clothes against your skin. The temperature of the air on your face. The pressure of your feet on the floor. The weight of your hands.
3 things you can hear. Go quiet enough to hear them. The hum of something electrical. A bird. Your own breathing.
2 things you can smell. This takes a moment. Coffee, laundry, the particular scent of this room.
1 thing you can taste. Whatever is present in your mouth right now.

Take another slow breath. The anxiety may not be gone, but your attention has moved. That is the point: to give the nervous system something concrete and present to register, without requiring the feeling to disappear first. Repeat the sequence if needed.

The 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique is widely used in cognitive behavioural therapy and trauma-informed practice, and has become standard in anxiety management education. It is considered public domain within the therapeutic tradition. The version here is a straightforward application without modification.
